questionText: The marks, $X$, scored by candidates in an examination are modelled by $X \sim N(58, 14^2)$. Find the mark below which $30\%$ of candidates score (the $30$th percentile), giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the condition
steps.explanation: Marks are normal with mean $\mu = 58$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 14$. The $30$th percentile is the mark $a$ with $30\%$ of candidates below it, so $P(X < a) = 0.30$.

steps.description: Recognise this as a lower-tail probability
steps.explanation: Because the condition is already written as an area to the left of $a$, the inverse-normal function can be applied directly with no rearranging.
The standardised value with $30\%$ of the area to its left is $z = -0.5244$. It is negative because a mark below which only $30\%$ fall must sit to the left of the mean.
Standardising the unknown mark $a$ should reproduce this $z$-value, so we set the standardised expression equal to it.
Multiplying both sides by $\sigma$ and adding $\mu$ makes $a$ the subject — the general rule for converting a $z$-value back into a mark.
Putting in the mean $58$, the standard deviation $14$ and the $z$-value $-0.5244$ gives the mark in the original units.
Carrying out the arithmetic gives a mark of about $50.66$.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures

steps.explanation: The mark lies below the mean of $58$, which makes sense: if only $30\%$ of candidates score below it — fewer than half — it must sit to the left of the average mark.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $a \approx 50.7$ (3 s.f.) — the mark below which $30\%$ of candidates score.
